column6:
That's not really a refutation. The fact is the indirect "benefit" is there. You can use your bias to say that's too bad, but the fact is the difference exists, as do many others which may favor one side or another.

I would say there's no reason this is supposed to be fair anyways.

You are conflating two separate issues.

ISSUE 1: Who should support the child? ANSWER: In the best interests of the child, both parents should support the child.

ISSUE 2: Should the law permit a woman to have an abortion? ANSWER: Embedded in her right to privacy, a woman should be able to make decisions over matters concerning her body, including the decision to abort a fetus, unless and until that right to privacy is outweighed by society's interest in the viability of the fetus. (This is why we have laws that restrict late term abortion.)

Those are the two issues at hand, and neither of them involve any unfairness to the potential father or leave any room for consideration of some right to deny support.

Oh heavens, honestly! This whole thing is so needlessly complicated. It feels like people are trying to twist everything into some sort of moral crisis. @absolute-effusion, you're right to call it out. It's not about fairness, it's about a messy situation with no easy answers. And @indigo-volt, while I understand the points about privacy and viability, it still feels like a huge overreach to dictate such a deeply personal decision. I just wish people would be able to move past this instead of getting so caught up in the arguments. Seriously frustrating.

column6:
>Should I really be any less proud of being born tall than being born with the ability to easily make effort?

A) Those two aren’t anywhere near the same thing. You shouldn’t be proud of your height. As far as I know, nothing in my DNA gives one the ability to work hard.

B) I, and many other people, find it incredibly difficult to work hard. I can’t concentrate for long periods of time, and hate having to work hard. But I’ve put in effort, and through that, I’ve made many accomplishments all of which I’m proud of. And if you come last, and you got there, despite putting in your hardest effort, despite trying as hard as you can, then it’s fair to say that you should be proud of yourself. Your achievement isn’t coming last, it’s all the effort you’ve put in.

You’re making it sound as if Usain Bolt didn’t put any effort into running fast. Usain Bolt has said before that what he has has come from hard work. And he should be proud of his hard work. Other people can be just as proud. It’s not a question of the degree to which he has worked hard, as coming second through hard work is just as worthy of pride as coming first. 

The difference is, is that being how you are born, has not come through hard work, it’s just how you are, and thus, deserves no pride.

column6:
Thank you!

While I still don't quite understand transgender identity, these resources will be helpful to seeing transition as beneficial from a health standpoint. ∆

Transition [**vastly reduces suicide risk**](http://www.torontosun.com/2015/06/08/suicide-rate-much-higher-for-transgender-canadians-study). The farther along in transition a trans person is, the lower the suicide risk becomes. After transition, and when spared discrimination and abuse, the rate of suicide attempts among trans people people are about the same as the national average. The ability to transition, along with family and social acceptance, are the [**largest factors reducing suicide risk among trans people**](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3722435/).

There are a [***lot***](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24344788) of [**studies**](http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2Fs10508-009-9551-1) showing that [**transition**](https://mayoclinic.pure.elsevier.com/en/publications/hormonal-therapy-and-sex-reassignment-a-systematic-review-and-met) [**improves**](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/tswj/2014/960745/) [**mental health**](http://europepmc.org/abstract/med/25690443) and [**quality of life**](http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10508-014-0453-5) while [**reducing dysphoria**](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/23553588_Long-term_Assessment_of_the_Physical_Mental_and_Sexual_Health_among_Transsexual_Women). 

Not to mention [**this 2010 meta-analysis**](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1365-2265.2009.03625.x/abstract) of 28 different studies, which found that transition is extremely effective at reducing dysphoria and improving quality of life.

The claim that transition does not dramatically reduce suicide risk is a deliberately dishonest misrepresentation of [**this study**](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3043071/), popularized by Paul McHugh, a religious extremist and [**leading member**](https://thinkprogress.org/hate-group-masquerading-as-pediatricians-attacks-transgender-youth-544e755c6a20) of an anti-gay and anti-trans [**hate group**](http://www.snopes.com/americas-pediatricians-gender-kids/), who presents himself as a reputable source but publishes work without peer review. His claim to fame is having shut down the Johns Hopkins trans health program in the 70's, which he did not based on medical evidence but on his personal ideological opposition to transition. Johns Hopkins has [**resumed offering transition related medical care**](http://transadvocate.com/johns-hopkins-resumes-trans-care_n_19077.htm), including reconstructive surgery, and their faculty are finally [**disavowing**](http://www.baltimoresun.com/news/opinion/oped/bs-ed-lgbtq-hopkins-20160928-story.html) him for his irresponsible and ideologically motivated misrepresentation of the current science of sex and gender.

That study's lead author Dr. Dhejne had [**emphatically denounced**](http://www.transadvocate.com/fact-check-study-shows-transition-makes-trans-people-suicidal_n_15483.htm) McHugh and his misuse of her work. Her study found only that trans patients who transitioned prior to 1989 had a somewhat higher risk of suicide attempts as compared to the *general public.* These rates were still far lower than the rates of suicide attempts among trans people prior to transition, and the authors of the article specifically identified the higher rates of abuse abuse and discrimination trans people suffered 27+ years ago as the source of greater risk of suicide among this population.

Dr. Dhejne's study found no difference in rates of suicide attempts between trans people who transitioned after 1989, and the general public.

This overwhelming evidence for the efficacy and necessity of transition, is why it is the only treatment for dysphoria recommended and recognized as an effective by all major US and world medical and psychological authorities.

* Here is the [**American Psychiatric Association's policy statement**](http://www.apa.org/about/policy/transgender.aspx) regarding the necessity and efficacy of transition as the appropriate treatment for gender dysphoria. More information from the APA [**here**](http://www.apa.org/pi/lgbt/programs/transgender/?tab=1).

* Here is a [**resolution from the American Medical Association**](http://www.tgender.net/taw/ama_resolutions.pdf) on the efficacy and necessity of transition as appropriate treatment for gender dysphoria, and call for an end to insurance companies categorically excluding transition-related care from coverage.

* [**Here**](http://hrc-assets.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com//files/documents/SupportingCaringforTransChildren.pdf) are the guidelines from the American Academy of Pediatrics.

* [**Here**](http://www.aafp.org/dam/AAFP/documents/about_us/special_constituencies/2012RCAR_Advocacy.pdf) is a similar resolution from the American Academy of Family Physicians.

* [**Here**](http://www.socialworkers.org/da/da2008/finalvoting/documents/Transgender%202nd%20round%20-%20Clean.pdf ) is one from the National Association of Social Workers.

* [**Here**](http://www.teni.ie/attachments/14767e01-a8de-4b90-9a19-8c2c50edf4e1.PDF) are the treatment guidelines from the Royal College of Psychiatrists, and [**here**](http://www.wlmht.nhs.uk/wp-content/uploads/2013/05/Gender-dysphoria-guide-for-GPs-and-other-healthcare-staff.pdf) are guidelines from the NHS. More from the NHS [**here**](http://www.nhs.uk/Conditions/Gender-dysphoria/Pages/Treatment.aspx).

column6: ...you're kidding right? It's an EXPLOSIVE DEVICE. This is a ridiculous argument. Not changing my view with this one, pal.

Do you believe that people should be allowed to own as much plastic explosive as they want?  
     
Plastic explosives are safer than guns.   
-It cannot be detonated without a blasting cap.  
-It will not detonate due to impact.  
-It will not detonate because of heat   

There are a myriad of productive uses:
-C4 can be consumed as a recreational(though dangerous drug)  
-It can be burned for heat  
-It can be used for safe destruction of trees, buildings, etc.  
-It can be used for self-defense   
-It is common in many industries  
  
However, people are not allowed to stockpile as much plastic explosive as they want. Why not?
Why are plastic explosives different from guns?

Well I'll be hornswoggled. This is absolutely ridiculous. Saying plastic explosives are safer than guns is monumentally ignorant. As a journalist, I've seen enough of this kind of dangerous rhetoric to last me a lifetime. And to suggest it can be used for recreational drug use?! Seriously?! @scalding-siege, you need to seriously reevaluate your judgment. This isn't a philosophical debate, it's about public safety. And frankly, it's disgusting.

@average-leopard I appreciate your concern for public safety, but let's keep the discussion factual and respectful. The original question was about the regulation of plastic explosives, not their recreational use. Let's focus on the safety and regulatory aspects.

column6:
>Most people in the US who don't use their vacation, do so because it is discouraged and just results in more work on return.

This is actually false. This is a premise promoted by people with a political agenda rather than using facts and logic. If you actually look at personality scores across countries, you'll find that the USA is actually one of the most conscientious countries in the world. Conscientiousness as a personality trait means essentially that you're industrious, and judgmental. Essentially the culture is a result of biology, the reason why you see on average less vacation times taken is because most people in the US are more conscientious than other people such as those in Europe and aren't predisposed to take breaks. I'm on mobile right now and I don't have the link itself, but the study was done by David P Schmidt, and has over 800 citations, which is quite a bit for a paper.

I've worked as a team manager for quite some time now and there are many times where I've had to force people to take breaks because they will over work themselves. This might be anecdotal, but I hear this a lot from people who are trying to be good managers and they find it difficult because they're having to ensure that their employees take even the minimum amount of breaks in order to increase their productivity.

>My argument or view I guess is a more constructive way of putting it, is that because of all of the issues I mentioned above, and the size of America, that America just is not as nice to live in as some other countries unless your primary concern is accumulating wealth.

I guess that depends on what your goals are. I'm happy seeing my family, doing interesting work, and living a simple life. I don't want the constant surveillance of Britain. I don't want to overbearing health regulations in the EU. I don't want the constant heat of Australia. I don't measure my quality of life by luxury cars or expensive vacations. I measure it by doing things I want to do.

Wow, okay dude. That's kinda a lot to unpack. I guess I guess I kinda get what you mean about not wanting to spend all my money on stuff I don't need. But it feels like people are always judging you for not being rich or doing crazy stuff. It's just... exhausting sometimes. @average-leopard you seem really sure about this study tho. What exactly did it say? 🤔

> I think when you look at mainstream political parties, media, the fact that racial statistics are not being conducted anymore by goverments who are becoming racially/ethnically diverse and just my experience and probably yours as well is evidence enough.

I don't think so. Lack of something is not evidence. I can't be sure that your experience excludes awareness of censuses that calculate racial statistics. I'm not familiar with a place like Germany's census system, so I wouldn't pretend to know what they have and haven't done without closer scrutiny.

[And this trend is being studied](http://www.pewforum.org/2015/04/02/europe/). The last census of Europe was in 2011 on order from the EU, and they calculated racial demographics.

>I mean another guy also just asked me open endedly why I think it would be a disaster, even though this is not about an evaluation of that, but of people like him who think that.

I can't parse this. How does this answer the challenge that you haven't provided justification for why people are okay with this. Please clearly describe the attitude you are arguing against and maybe what you would replace that attitude with.

column6:
Not really, for three reasons:

1) I'm not actually arguing that it's okay because of what OP does that's similar.  I'm pointing to OP's own experience and asking them to question whether their implicit assumptions about lawful conduct are reasonable.  If OP can look at their own experience and see how unreasonable it is to view the law as they do, the argument works.

2) I'm demonstrating that OP's position is inconsistent with their own behavior in the current moment, not just the past.  They can still adhere to the law and choose to adhere to it in the future and their position and actions will be perfectly consistent.  If not, they will be a hypocrite.  That doesn't mean the idea is wrong in and of itself, but it's often hard to consciously hold two contradictory ideas to be true simultaneously.

The subreddit is called ChangeMyView, not ProveMeWrong.

3) I'm demonstrating the practical consequences of following the implicit assumptions that OP makes in their own life.  If the law should be as respected as OP says as a matter of principle, OP should modify their behavior in certain ways.  If they don't wish to do that, they would again be a hypocrite.

column6:
What’s the value in a clearly stated position that is neither researched nor likely to be his stated position in 5 minutes?

Clinton got drug through the mud for having a private opinion and a public opinion. But this is what good statesmanship requires. Do you tell your coworkers what you really think of them?

(As an aside, let’s say Trump survives the Mueller investigation largely unscathed, but the Dems somehow take the Senate in addition to the house. What are the chances Trump signs every piece of legislation the Dems put in front of him just to get positive press?  How confident are you of your answer?)

>No one ever has to wonder what the president is actually thinking or what his underlying motives are on a particular issue.

Are we looking at the same man?

Seriously? That's a monumentally stupid point. You think people are going to be able to parse through every single statement a president makes and figure out their *true* motivation? That's not how democracy works. And frankly, it's insulting to imply that people are so easily misled. @serious-conduction, you need to get a grip.

@linear-team: That's a fair point about democracy and the complexity of motivations. However, in a political discussion, it's often reasonable to question whether a leader's public statements align with their true intentions, especially when those intentions could affect public policy. Let's keep the discussion respectful and on topic.

Because if you don't acknowledge that you might be inclined to believe that the parts in life where you find success and others don't are due *entirely* to differences in character. column6:
I think its reasonable to look at FDR's internment of the Japanese during WWII, and conclude that this was a deplorable act during what was otherwise a sound and competent presidency.  FDR was a product of his time, and I think its fair to judge him as such.

Its not really clear to me where the "sound and competent" portion of Trump's presidency would come from.  His chief talent seems to be stealing the headlines, which I suppose might be useful in some hypothetical situation, but he doesn't seem to have any capacity at all for patient reasoned analysis, which is not something you would say of even a character like Nixon (he makes GWB look professorial by comparison, which is quite a feat).

Going back farther than FDR seems somewhat pointless to me, as the stakes of executive power simply weren't as high.  I'm not an expert on the presidency, but it seems like there were definitely some Trump-level buffoons who managed to snag the office in the 19th century.  Things moved so much slower back then, there was a lot more cushion to the world order.

column6:
What makes you think that Trump is the first batshit crazy, piece of shit, power hungry politician? There have been plenty, and lots of them take advantage of citizens feelings to accomplish their own goals. Fear is a big one. Look at Joseph Mccarthy and the red scare. What about FDR and the Japanese camps in WW2?

You have to realize that American citizens elect these people too. As much as politicians are to blame for taking advantage of peoples feelings, we have to give some blame to citizens too for not doing their homework and continuing to elect politicians who have continued to take away personal liberties (in some ways. In others, like gay and black rights, stuff has drastically improved) and have put our country $19 trillion in debt through irresponsible spending and money creation.

TLDR: Lots of politicians take advantage of citizens and abuse government power to further their own means. Most of America is too stupid to see their bullshit and tell them to fuck off and never elect them again.

column6:
The complete and utter lack of correlation between gun ownership and overall homicide rate suppors the substitution premise for homicde.

[Here is just something I picked out that illustrates the point clearly for US states.](http://blog.landonswan.com/post/45143952046/gun-control-vs-murdercrime-rates-a-statistical-look) Feel free to check the numbers, as they should be publicly available. [Here's one that covers OECD standard developed countries.](http://crimeresearch.org/2014/03/comparing-murder-rates-across-countries/) [This one shows the global scale stats.](http://www.realclearscience.com/blog/2013/03/guns-neither-increase-nor-decrease-crime-rate.html).

This is not true at all, and it's [even less true for suicides](http://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=92319314), which are the leading cause of firearm deaths. Eliminating a very easy way to kill someone (or yourself) does not mean that you're automatically going to find another means to do it. The statistics do not bear that out. If the easiest method isn't available, people then have to take the time to pursue another method. By the time they do that, they've often calmed down enough that they don't follow through.
Seriously?! You think I'm gonna believe that?! @vibrato-quaver you gotta be kidding me. This is rigged! 😡
